section_title: THE INDISPENSABLE NEED FOR A TRADING PLAN
content:
Hello folks, we are going to cover the need for a trading plan. If you're new to trading and you do not have a
trading plan, it's hopefully my goal as an end result in this presentation to communicate the need for one.
If you already started trading and you've been having difficulties and you don't have a trading plan, I could
pretty much guarantee if you just put some work into formulating a very clear, concise trading plan, your
results would be dramatically different. Basically, having no plan equals emotional driven results.
You're not going to have any understanding what to do, you're going to be reacting more than you are
anticipating, and that's the stark difference between a professional trader because they do have a detailed,
organized trading plan. It's well written, it's by their trading desk, it's memorized, it's reviewed continuously.
By having such, they have an objective end result and it really is the DNA of a successful trader. So having
a trading plan is at the top of your list as far as being a consistently profitable trader.

The majority of your time, what do you do as a trader and where is it most spent in terms of time? Well, first
you got to understand that you have to manage your time wisely. No one's going to manage it for you, so
it's important that you understand that the countless hours that you spend in analysis without having a
clear objective or premise to it, it's actually counterproductive. You feel like you're doing the right thing by
spending a lot of time staring at the chart, but unless you have an understanding exactly what it is you're
looking for, you're just wasting your time.
You need to spend quality time with the macroeconomic analysis and higher time frame support and
resistance levels. I can't stress this enough because most new traders come into the marketplace and they
spend too far time in the 5 and 15 minute chart and maybe even 1 minute charts hoping to see some kind
of mysterious neon sign saying 'buy me' or 'sell me'. Nothing significant occurs without first leaving telltale
signs on a long-term basis. That means if there's a significant price move, I guarantee you if you look at the
higher time frame charts
- and I'm referring to the monthly, weekly, daily, four-hour, and no less than a one
- hour chart
- those timeframes are going to have a clue as to what was the catalyst for that move prior to it
actually unfolding.
Follow the interest rates. This is where the majority of your time can be spent. You need to get a feel for
what interest rates are trying to do, are they moving higher, are they moving lower? Keeping track of the
overnight lending rate with the banks per country is absolutely significant information and insight you need
as a consistent trader.

What's the least important process in trading? Don't focus on the money, number one, and unfortunately
we all get into this business for a number of things. But the top of the list should be to make money, but
while you're trading your focus should not be on the money. Don't focus on the amount of money that you
hope to make. There's no guarantee you're going to make it on every trade, so there's no real point in
hoping. You just trade the process, trade your plan, and the end result should, if you have a well-grounded,
well-formulated trading plan, money will deposit into your account.
Do not worry about losses. You're gonna have losing trades, if you accept that going in, you won't be
demoralized from it. Every good trader, whether they're professional or not, they have losing streaks. Do not
worry about missed trades. I'm going to tell you right now, I'd probably miss more winning trades every
single week then most of you realize. I don't take a whole lot of trades, I like to cherry pick, I like to take
things that are really set up and if you have that mindset going in, much like expecting losses, it's very
comforting to know that you're not feeling rushed, and that's a very important characteristic of a
professional trader. We aren't rushed to get in the marketplace. We react slowly when trying to make
money and we react fast and quickly when we're trying to preserve our money. That's a very significant
paradigm shift that you're going to need if you don't already have that mindset going in.

The least important process in trading is the entry signals, and that probably shocked you right there, but it
really is least in terms of significance. If you don't have a macro understanding of what's going on in the
marketplace and the higher time frame directional bias, chances are you're probably gonna be wrong more
times than you're right. So it's my goal to really try to drive you to looking at the weekly, monthly, the daily,
and the 4-hour charts, and if you're gonna go a little less than four hour, don't go below one hour, but
spend most of your time on the weekly and daily. Those charts are gonna be the most in significance
because that's where the banking levels are determined.
Premise and sound basis is paramount. That means you have to have an understanding of what it is that
you're doing. You're not just staring blindly at charts because it's not going to get you anywhere. I
guarantee you, unless you have a specific pattern, a motive that you use to get into a particular trade and
you hunt that, whether you're back testing or walking forward with it, unless you have that understanding of
what it is specifically that you're looking for, you're just basically spinning your wheels and it's my advice not
to do that. Keep the focus on the plan and work the trading edge that your method exploits.

Trading plans are basically taking all the pieces of the puzzle to equate to successful trading and bringing
those pieces together. Those pieces could be fundamental data, technicals, intermarket analysis; all these
things go together in terms of building a trading plan.
- Impeccable risk control is the first component to any successful trading plan.
- Flawless equity management, which is closely tied to risk control, is essential to every successful trading
plan. You can have all the bells and whistles on your trade monitor, but it means nothing unless you control
your money and the risks involved in all your trades.
- Fundamentals at any measure can always assist a trader in trade plan development.
- Intermarket analysis is key to a well balanced trading plan. Understanding how all the asset classes tie
together and hinge upon the movement of the interest rate markets, that is crucial for your long-term
success and longevity in trading.
- Technical analysis and a comprehensive approach to sound tools and concepts is the framework to
exercising a trading plan. If you don't have a concise list of specific tools that you use for specific times and
reasons, you're going to be floating in and out with different oscillators, you're going to be using different
tools. Understand what tools you're going to have and stick to those and understand what it is that those
specific tools do and try not to use them for any other reason except for those purposes only.
- Executing a top-down analysis with all the above components will deliver optimal results while controlling
risk, which is the number one reason why we as traders last as long as we do in this business. We control
risk, so all the pieces together, it begins and ends with the control of risk.

The paradigm shift. Alright guys, you are not the only predator in the marketplace. I know sometimes when
we get these trading plans and trading tools, and this is the first time you've ever probably looked at
organized structure of how to apply analysis to the marketplace, and you feel kind of like a big-shot and
that's normal, that's kind of cool I know, but do not overestimate the importance of knowing there's other
people out there that want your money too. Whether you go in confident or you develop confidence later on,
understand overconfidence is actually counterproductive. Overconfidence is actually an invitation for ego,
which also allows the input of emotions and that's not what you want in your trading.
Never assume your well-designed trading plan won't hit a losing streak. I'm assuring you that it will, there's
no one out there that is immune to it. You're going to have a losing streak. Always reflect on your wins as a
victory and your losses as a learning opportunity. All learning opportunities that I've experienced in my
trading have actually produced greater growth in my development as a trader, so loss is actually a
beneficial thing. It will take preparation in advance to weather periods of inevitable drawdown. Journal your
results, both good and bad. It's important you go back to that journal because at periods of drawdown and
strings of losses, it will help remind you that these are temporary events and that you do eventually trade
out of them if you control risk and reduce the overall exposure as you continue to take losses.
Do not overdrive your trading plan or attempt to trade outside its intended purpose. Do not turn a position
trade into multiple different time frame trades. If your analysis suggests it's a position trade, trade it like that.
Every great trader has one thing in common and it's the respect and understanding that the risk will always
outweigh the reward unless it is properly managed. It's not about the profits, it's controlling risk.

section_title: "PACK SMALL, PLAY BIG" (2 PERCENT RISK OR LESS)
content:
Pack small, play big. 2 percent risk or less is it really enough? I probably get this question emailed to me
more than any other. When you get into trading guys, you probably fantasize about going to places like this
and you think you need to risk 20, 15, 10 percent of your account to get there. And I can assure you, I've
been to places like this and I've done it with 2 percent risk or many times with less than that, and it's
amazing if you just understand the power of compound interest, you don't need a whole lot of risk exposure
to build this wealth.
The question will always be in the developing trader's mind, how can I make a fortune with so little risk?
And hopefully by the end of this presentation you'll see that you don't really need a whole lot of risk, you
just need time and consistency. Proficient, consistent traders focus on the reality that their trading will not
be perfect. We are not going in with the mindset expecting our trades to be perfect. We don't even need
high accuracy, but you do need consistently following a trading plan, that's paramount. You have to
continuously do that. You can make all the money you ever need risking 2 percent and many times even
less than that per trade. Wealth is built with time, not excessive risk exposure.

Keeping realistic goals in focus. Alright guys, you've seen all of the internet gurus and you probably thought
I was one like that as well that promised riches real quick, you're going to get a thousand pips every month.
Guys look, don't take the bait, that is meant for you to lose. Do not buy into that mindset. Your goal is to
steadily build your account with little drawdown as humanly possible. Your trading is not hinged on the
premise you must hit home run profits every trade or even weekly. There's a lot of times I'll have a losing
week, there's many times I'll have a losing day and then come back from that and be able to recoup it, but
I'm not always hitting win-win-win-win-win and you're not going to either.
Your career should be built on a low yet sufficient baseline target based on 1 percent return or pips per
month or week. Trading with risk controlled and profiting a net gain of 25 pips per week should be a new
trader's goal. After consistently harvesting 25 pips per week, graduate to targeting 50 pips. After 50 pips
per week is achieved, aim for 75 pips and eventually move beyond that as your experience and tolerance
for risk increases.

section_title: CASE STUDY 1: 25 PIPS PER WEEK (6 PERCENT MONTHLY RETURN)
content:
Let's look at a case study here. We're going to look at a trading model that more or less targets and shoots
a goal for 25 pips for the week, and we're looking at possibly making 6 percent return for the month. We're
risking 2 percent per trade and 30 pips tops are used and the monthly goal only equates to 90 pips for the
entire month.
Now if you look at these numbers, okay, this is a spreadsheet so there's gonna be a little bit of rounding
issue, but 23 pips per week, roughly we'll just call 25 pips, that would equate to 6 percent return per month.
If you start with $2,500, in one year your account goes to over $5,000, which is more than doubling. After 2
years it goes to $10,122. So you see, essentially you're doubling your money every single year, you're only
risking 2 percent and you're only looking for 25 pips per week.
Now notice over here we have daily column. This is more or less to stimulate the idea that if you trade on a
daily basis and you scalp
- I don't really teach traders that go in and try to scalp 5 pips
- but just for the
sake of argument, we're gonna look at every day if you scalped 5 pips, that would give you your 25 pips for
the week. Obviously, if you're trading the best days of the Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday, regardless
of what pair you trade, 8 pips per day three times a week would give you your goal for the week, and
obviously 11 should be around rough round up like 12 twice a week would give you your weekly pip haul.
So you can see over time your money is growing steadily and while it's not a whole lot of money, you got to
look at what you're starting with and what you're really aiming for, and I think 25 pips per week if you're a
brand new trader is an absolutely wonderful goal to start with.

section_title: CASE STUDY 2: 50 PIPS PER WEEK (10 PERCENT MONTHLY RETURN)
content:
Look at another case study, 50 pips per week. We're gonna be targeting 10 percent per month starting with
the same account $2,500. Now this time we're gonna exercise the risk exposure of 1.5 percent, 30 pips per
stop, and the monthly goal in terms of pips is 200 pips and the weekly goal is 50 pips per week.
If you trade every day scalping 10 pips per day equates to 50 pips. And three times Tuesday, Wednesday,
and Thursday looking for 17 pips per day or two times for the week at 25 pips per trade. The first year your
account would move to $7,846 and then in second year it would be at $24,624. Not bad, and you're not
really hitting home runs yet guys, are you? It's only 50 pips.

section_title: CASE STUDY 3: 75 PIPS PER WEEK (10 PERCENT MONTHLY RETURN)
content:
Look at a case study where we're looking for 75 pips per week. Now I can tell you, you can build a career
on this, 75 pips is absolutely astonishing if you use it the right way. 10 percent per month risking only 1
percent per trade using a 30 pips stop loss. The goal 300 pips, 75 pips per week. If you day trade every
single day, 15 pips would be your goal, 25 pips if you're trading 3 times per week, and 38 pips twice a week
would equate to your goal. And in one year, your $2,500 would essentially grow to $7,846, again $24,624.

section_title: MODULAR THINKING AND FLEXIBLE GOAL SETTING
content:
Modular thinking, steady progress. Now small consistency can still surprise you. It takes an exponential
mindset, little things over a period of time equals big results. Building your trading plan flexibility. You're not
always going to see it on the timeline that you want. You might have a weekly goal that may require shorter
term day trades or scalps to achieve. Do not force yourself into hunting or believing you need to make your
goals daily and or weekly. Your weekly goals might fall short a week or two, but still might be met in the
course of the same month. It may just average out guys. Your monthly goal might average out over the
quarter or over the entire year. Don't be so rigid and allow the market to pay you when and how it will.
Your goals might fall short of your forecast and hopes, however do not be disappointed if this occurs. You
will still be heading in the right direction and that's really what matters.

Success without homeruns. Is it possible? Well let's look at this, this is kind of lofty I admit, but this is what
could happen, 20 percent per month. And it's rather impressive, oh no that's just that's another that you've
many fantasize about. We're going to be risking 2 percent per trade maximum and now we have an
understanding what we knew using our trading plan and we have some consistency, we understand how to
use a lower timeframe so we can use now 20 pip stop losses. The monthly goal is 200 pips, still in terms of
many expectations of traders that's very, very modest. Weekly pip goal 50 pips, and if you break that down
it could be done over a course of every day at 10 pips, three times a week at 17 pips, and 25 times 2 per
week. $2,500 in one year grows to $22,290 and in two years it goes to $198,000 and in 34 months it goes
to what everyone fantasizes about in trading, the 1 million dollar mark.

Keep something. Pay yourself. You have to learn to be methodical about taking first profit guys. No one's
gonna pay you, your broker's not going to take first profit for you. They're not gonna scale out some of your
position and make sure you get some profit out of a winning trade, it's your responsibility. So before taking
any trade, it's crucial for you to know at what level you will take first profit and then start to reduce or
remove risk altogether.
Limiting risk after taking half of your original position off will remove emotional and psychological pressures
you may feel while the position is open. If you take first profit, that will absolutely remove all of the rush for
you to be able to close the trade because how many times you've been on a trade, even in a demo account
if you guys are new, and you see a profit and you just want to close it out because you just want to see it in
your account as registering as a profit. When you take something off and you split your position in half, take
some of it off, move your stop to break-even or reduce it to half of what your original profit was, that's a
wonderful opposition to begin because now no matter what, you either make half of your first position's
profit potential, in other words first profit, or you stop out of even and you collect your first profit and that
was it.

Taking first profit at a predetermined level will assist in building confidence and removes the sting of the
trade losing should it reverse on you and stop you at a break-even. How many times you had a winning
trade and then go against you and become a losing trade? I know that's like, I'm not immune to that, I
experienced that. The greatest thing you can do is learn to take profits, take first profit and scale.

Continuing our theme, success without homeruns. Now let's take a look at an example. Let's assume we
are a trader that now uses two positions to enter on a trade. In other words, we take a maximum 2 percent
risk and we split it in half. The first portion of our trade, we take first profit at 30 pips. We're going to be
risking 20 pips, so we're already risking 1.5 to 1 reward to risk. So we're making a little bit more than what
we're risking for the initial first profit. And if we just did that alone, and the second portion of our trade we
move to break-even and it stops out every single time we trade for the entire year, our account would still
double. Risking 1 percent it would equate to 6 percent, so that's still good.
But now let's look what happens if we allow the second portion of our trade to run. What happens if the
second portion captures the average daily range? Now the average daily range for this sake of argument,
we're just going to use is 100 pips. Now that would equate to if you just did this once per week. Now don't
look at the daily, the three times per week or twice a week numbers, we'll block that out here, but the
weekly number that 100, that's going to more or less equate to one solid trade for the week where you nail
down one average daily range or collect one hundred pips, in other words, in terms of an intraday trade,
that would equate to 20 percent.
Now think, 20 percent plus 6 percent, what are you making? We'll get that in a moment. But if we're able to
do this, our money with 1 percent risk grows exponentially. Now taking both of those examples together
and assuming ideal scenarios at the end or both are profitable, that would equate to about 26 percent
return. Your $2,500 would grow to $40,000 in one year and $640,000 in two years. Notice that you're really
not even risking more than $3.20 average per pip.

Alright guys, interest rates, that is the market's driving force. If you've never learned about interest rates,
you're gonna learn a little bit about it in this presentation. But what makes the markets go around, whether
it be stocks, commodities, Forex, it's all interest rates. You need to get to know the interest rate market
because that will be the key to you unlocking the mind. The entire global markets are interconnected and
they spin on the gyrations in the interest rate market.
Each country we trade currencies with has their own interest rates and thus these rates influence the
demand or disenchantment with its own currency. The market will always seek yield, and the higher the
yield, the bigger demand on average. There are several types of interest rates that traders can track and
utilize to determine a trade idea. Understanding how they trade and the yields will make long term trends
easier to determine.

Interest rate spreads and differentials. Alright guys, you need to track the country's overnight lending rates.
That's one of the core principles to having long term trend analysis. And if you just Google any country and
look for historical data, you can get that. Keep a spreadsheet and you can do a whole lot with that
information. It's not my goal to do that here, you can find sources on the internet to actually do these for
you. But the currency markets, they more or less trend on long term basis based on interest rates and
understanding which country has higher interest rates and which have lower rates can be a differential play
or carry trade. And not only can you trade profit by this directional bias in your trading, but it also could be
paid interest on top of that.
If the country sees lower interest rates, this will weaken the base currency. On the contrary, if the rates are
increasing, this will prop up the country's currency.

Million dollar futures insights, seasonal tendencies. Now some of you folks have already been introduced to
this graph. This is the British Pound seasonal tendency. This is a chart that I've held dear to me for many
years, since 1995. I got this from Larry Williams' course and it was among other seasonal tendencies, but
because I've grown to trade the British Pound more than any other pair, this seasonal tendency chart is
kind of like as it gives you like a macro roadmap of what should transpire.
Now one of the most impressive concepts you can learn is the nature of seasonal tendencies in the
marketplace. The commodity market is one such market that has a very cyclical to seasonal tendencies
that have an uncanny ability to forecast major price swings. Now I have used seasonal tendencies for
nearly 20 years and they still amaze me. One such seasonal tendency is the British Pound to make a major
low in February to March, June, September, October, and December, and major highs in April to May. This
is also seen in stocks as well.

Let's look at the comparison of the British Pound and since we've already learned that the interest rate
market is what more or less pushes the markets all around, we're gonna look at the US Treasuries, yes it
does have a seasonal tendency. Now I want to draw your attention to the fact that usually in April and May,
if you look at the British Pound chart on the left, you can see that there's a red line and a blue line. And
what that is, is it's measuring the bullish years and the bearish years together to get a composite index,
and the blue line is a 25 year historical data and the red line is a 15 year historical data, so you can see
over overall basically the seasonal tendency is pretty solid.
It usually makes a rally of some kind late February, early March, and trades up into the April May time
period where it makes a significant high and trades down into the June timeframe. Well, if the bonds are
inverse, in other words as currencies go down, the bond market or the futures market will go up, and while
the bonds go up, the yield is inverted so the yield would be going down while the bond goes up. And for
instance, let's look at the April May time period on the bond and you can see that it has seasonal tendency
to rally that would indicate that interest rates would be going down. Now if interest rates are going down,
that would drive what? Currencies lower. There won't be any demand, so what will they be doing? It'll be a
flight to quality that will rally the dollar, and that would be essentially a risk off scenario.
Now look at January and February in the bond market. We see the bonds are sliding down into that April
May time period, that means that interest rates would be increasing. Now if interest rates are increasing,
that's going to have demand for currency and it'll be a risk on scenario. The dollar index will be falling at
that time period. Look at January, February time period and you can see we're in the 15-year basis it was
making lows, and even in the 25 year basis it was making lows where it did rally up and essentially around
the March time period it got in sync and makes its low and then they'll trade up in the dead spring time
period making a high. So study these charts and you can see there's a huge advantage of having insight
like this because it can put you on one track to finding a long-term trend and this is waiting for interest rates
to get in sync with this.

CoT and commercial trends, tracking the smart money. Okay guys, you've probably seen this a few times in
some other tutorials, but mainly you want to be looking at higher timeframe charts and you want to spend
some time analyzing the net positions of the large commercial traders. You want to be looking at the trend
of where their net positions are moving, are they continuously moving higher, low, or higher, lower, overall
bearish or bullish, and low? Is it already swinging bullish above the baselines of your line during an
uptrend, or are they doing that doing downtrend? And why would you be looking at the higher time frame
weekly chart and determine if the price is going higher? And if it is, you want to be waiting for the
commercials to be net long or extremely lightening up on their net short positions because that would
equate to a bullish scenario.
The trend on the weekly chart and the swings from net long to net short are very easy to spot and
determine the long to intermediate term trends or direction. Looking for trades in this trend or directional
bias will further increase your odds of capturing trades with large institutional sponsorship behind the
moves.

Trading the news. Alright, I basically look at trading the news or news releases as volatility injections. I don't
fear news, but I certainly respect it. There are certain reports and events that I more or less will step
outside of and more trade. But one of the most difficult ways to trade the markets is to try to trade the news
releases themselves. The Internet is littered with so-called robots or programs designed to quickly give you
trading profits on the knee-jerk reactions to the news. To me, this is pure and simple gambling. We can
never know for sure what the numbers will be in the reports, and the guess is just ridiculous.
However, we can wait for the release and watch the reaction, and many times a signal will form shortly after
the release. Many times counter direction to the intended news. Hawks use news releases for injections
and volatility and fuel trade ideas based on price action.

Key market moving indicators, key economic releases. Okay, we're gonna be looking at the US market, the
UK market and the German market. Okay, we're gonna start with the UK. The British Pound:
- Daily rate decision
- Retail sales
- Consumer prices
- Claimant count
- GDP
- Industrial production
Those are reports that I have on my calendar, times when they're going to come out, and I will absolutely
be aware of these and I will be more or less looking for trades to either be propelled in the direction that I've
been prior to the report, or I'll be looking for a signal to generate shortly after one of these reports are
released.

Alright, the US Dollar, we're going to the North American market:
- Federal Reserve rates
- Retail sales
- Consumer and producer prices
- Non-farm payroll (I avoid that, I do not touch it)
- Gross domestic product (GDP)
- Trade balance
- Consumer confidence reports
- Service and manufacturing ISM
You want to note these as well because all of your crosses with the dollar will be impacted by this as well.

Okay, the German market, look at the Euros:
- Rate decision
- German Ifo
- German unemployment
- German consumer prices
- German GDP
- Manufacturing and services sector PMI
You want to have these on your calendar when time they're gonna be released and be aware of these
because these absolutely will move the market considerably.

section_title: PLANNING FOR NEWS IMPACT AND RECOMMENDED RESOURCES
content:
I plan for news impact. You have to have an awareness of your economic calendar folks. Now there are a
few resources that you can use to determine when high impact news will release and hit the market:
- Number one, obviously babypips.com's calendar
- Forex Factory.com calendar
- You can actually Google 'econ a day calendars', you can find some that Bloomberg and a few other
resources but they have a very good format and I like that they're very user friendly.
And for more information on this topic, and if you have any more interest on interest rates, I would
recommend that Chris Lori's 'Inside the Banks' webinar. I purchased this a number of years ago and I was
very impressed with his presentation on the banking level in terms of interest rates and how they move and
propel the marketplace. So if this doesn't, if this doesn't more or less get to the understanding you were
looking for, I would counsel you to do a little investment in your career and it's not much money at all but it
absolutely is a wonderful resource. He does a really good job in that webinar. You can reach Chris Lori at
ChrisLori.com and you can get his webinar from his website. He's actually the only one that I recommend
any folks buy material from. I have everything but his Aussie Yen course and I just don't trade yen crosses,
otherwise I probably bought that as well. He does a really good job of teaching, he's very, very interested in
your success and that separates him as far as my concern from all the other folks out there, so kudos to
him.

section_title: 30-YEAR TREASURIES AND T-NOTES: UNLOCKING MARKET INSIGHT
content:
Alright guys, 30 year Treasuries and T-notes. Now some of my best concepts are found in my early days of
the futures trader, and the bond market is a big market and it provides so much insight and leading
information. Now I don't trade bonds anymore but I absolutely monitor their futures on a daily basis. This
market can tell you everything you need to know in any market asset class if you understand it. The scope
of the bond market is not the objective here, but we will use it to discover some rather impressive
techniques to ferret out some blockbuster trades.

section_title: TREASURY NOTES (10-YEAR, 5-YEAR, 2-YEAR): YIELD INVERSION AND TRACKING
content:
Ten-year Treasury notes. Much like the 30-year Treasury bond, I used these early on in my career as a
futures trader. And there's a little caveat to understand the bond market. Whenever you see the price chart
on the futures, when they rally up, they will have inverse relationship, in other words, as the price data goes
higher, the yield will actually be going down, so you have to have that in your mind. Now since I trade the
cable and the fiber exclusively, I trade tracking the German ten-year bond as well.
The five-year Treasury note. Much like the 10 year and 30 year, when price runs up on the contract price
on the futures market, the yield will be basically decreasing or dropping, and obviously if the chart would be
dropping on the futures that would mean the yield would be increasing as well. And much like the 10 year, I
also tracked the 5-year German bond as well. And 2-year Treasury note, much like the 5 year, 10 year, I
track that on the German basis as well. And by having any understanding of all three of those, we can find
some pretty neat things we're going to learn about here shortly.

section_title: US DOLLAR INDEX (USDX) AND SMT CONCEPTS
content:
Obviously, it goes without saying, the U.S. Dollar Index. I like dailyfx.com. If you go to the net dania charting
- now do not go to netdaniel.com
- it's very important, go to dailyfx.com and use the net dania charting. It's
free, and just open up a US dollar index chart and it'll give you what I'd like to see in terms of the U.S. dollar
index. I look at the cash basis also, and the chart to the right here, this is the futures chart of United States
dollar index. If you like these types of charts and you want to subscribe to them, it's not very expensive, all
things like 25 dollars a month, but you can get that at pricecharts.com. You don't need to pay for futures
charts, but I do prefer this format because I'm used to reading them since 1990s, I just grown accustomed
to having them in my repertoire. Now I like to use SMT concepts to spot diverging highs and lows between
the USDX and relative price points in the fiber and cable, and you knew that as my USDX SMT, and I look
for the bond and USDX SMT as well.

section_title: MAJOR STOCK MARKET INDICES AS CLUES
content:
Major stock market indices. Okay, the major stock market indices are one of my favorite tools to gauge long
- term price swings. If we are failing at highs on the daily charts as we see here in the image to the right, this
suggests a possible risk off scenario. Expect weaker stock prices and possibly foreign currencies. The U.S.
dollar index will confirm this with a rally at support at the same juncture. Simply reverse for risk on
scenarios. The markets as a whole are interconnected and it pays to monitor them and hunt for clues.

section_title: CRB COMMODITY INDEX FOR MARKET ANALYSIS
content:
The CRB Commodity Index. Okay, now I usually watch this on the cash basis and you can get that from the
pricecharts.com. I'm sure I can do a Google, you'll probably find it as well. But again, I really like this chart, I
think it's real crisp and when you print them out, there's nothing like printing out charts and marking them
up by hand. I'm like a Flintstone caveman, I like being able to carve up some notes and draw over my
charts. I like sharing with you guys electronically, but if it's going to be a matter of preference, I would love
to have printed charts every single day, that's just my opinion really.
Now I like the CRB index because it usually has an inverse relationship to the dollar index and I like to
confirm a swing higher in dollar with shorting fiber and or cable with a CRB decline. Now the CRB index
tends to be a little bit early and can warn of possible long-term shifts in the trend. It's a dynamite tool to use
for major market analysis and pinning down long to intermediate term directional bias.

section_title: OIL AND GOLD AS RISK BAROMETERS
content:
Alright, oil and gold. Now I like looking at these two markets, I don't trade them but I'd like to use them as
barometers more or less to see if there's risk-on, risk-off scenarios underway. And basically you want to be
looking at the same way that foreign currencies and CRB, all those markets should be moving in tandem
with both.

section_title: T-NOTE YIELD DIVERGENCES AND BUY/SELL PROGRAMS (CASE STUDY)
content:
Okay, we are looking at the T-note yields. These are going to be the two-year, five-year, and ten-year. And
you can look at the legend up here to determine which is which as we go through. And what I'm
highlighting here is when there is a divergence between these yields. These are T-note yields and this line
is not the T-note price, it's the actual T-note yield, so the yield is actually inverse, it's an inversion of the
futures chart's price of the T-note. So I kind of monitor both, but the yield when you monitor it like this, it will
basically mirror what foreign currencies should be doing. In other words, as the yields were increasing
here, that would have been bullish for foreign currency and contrary as the yields drop, that would be
bearish for foreign currencies. Okay, you see these confirmations in your technicals on your charts.
In other words, there's a buy signal for foreign currencies in your daily charts or respective timeframes that
you trade, and there is a sell signal being confirmed in the US dollar, that is a confirmation that you've
probably got a good probability of a nice swing for your respective time frame. Now what we're referring to
here is a daily basis, so this is really going to give you a buy program or sell program. This is the
beginnings of a buy program, this is determination of a buy program and a beginning of a sell program.
Now the main thing is I want you to focus on is that there's typically a three or four month cycle. It's like
almost like a quarterly effect that takes place in the yields and it's based basically on interest rates and
macroeconomics and things of that nature, but I'm not going to bore you with that here.
But just basically, if you look at the charts and on a historical perspective you'll see that that is in fact what
occurs on the average. And we're going to talk about, at the time of this recording, this is June 2012, and
what I'm looking at is trying to go over why we saw the precipitous slide in the foreign currency market,
namely the British Pound and the Euro USD pairs, cable and fiber. And we're gonna look at how that
relates to these yields here. Okay, and what we're looking at again on this chart is the United States ten
year, the United States five year, and the United States two year T-notes. Okay, and again these are the
yields and basically what you're looking for is at specific times, seasonally and quarterly, there will be a
divergence that posts in these yields, and that looks like this. We have, it may be a little difficult to see, I
understand, but the orange line here, which is the two year, the high made back here is lower than the high
it made in March, April time period. Or actually, in March 19th, the high here on the ten-year was just about
equal, and same thing here, we have the five year just about equal. Okay, so we have a divergence
between the two-year, five-year, and ten-year. Okay, that typically is a reason to start looking for other
reasons to support a decline in yields, which would represent a decline in foreign currency and a rally in the
U.S. dollar index. Okay, so let's take a look at a few other examples.

And we're gonna look at the foreign bond markets. Alright, now we're looking at the five-year notes on the
UK, I'm sorry, UK 5 year note, the German five-year note, and against the United States five-year Treasury
note. Okay, and we're gonna look at this same time frame in here. Going back to this chart real quick, just
for reference, again we're looking in 2012, March, April time period. Okay, we're gonna go and look at this
area here and what I want to draw your attention to is the fact that we had the U.S. five-year note higher
respectively while the German five-year note was lower and the UK five-year note was lower. Okay, and
what I'm gonna do is I'm gonna take off the UK so you can see there is a clear divergence between the
U.S. and the German that will sufficiently show the divergence. Okay, you can see the high here, too high
on the red line, that's the German five-year note, bearish, while at the same time the U.S. five-year note
was making a higher high in yield. Okay, and then you saw them slide together, that that is a yield SMT
divergence. Okay, as I look at it.
YouYou can see a contrary signal here back in October of 2010 where the German five-year note was
failing to make lower lows in the yield, at the same time the U.S. five-year note was making a lower low.
Okay, and then we saw both yields increase together. And here, the high, you saw an inability for the U.S.
to trade higher in yield while the German yield went higher and you can see there was the shift in the major
market sentiment, and that's really what this is measuring, market sentiment because you're you're chasing
the yield, and the market's always gonna seek yield. Okay, so if the yields are increasing, that's gonna be
bullish for major currencies and it's gonna be bearish for the dollar and vice versa.
And we've seen the same thing here in this April 2010. Okay, the U.S. five-year made higher highs while the
German five-year note was unable to make equivalent new highs, and there was your shift and notice this
is happening the March April time period. And we just recently at the time of this recording also had
another divergence which is a continuation of this longer-term downtrend in the yield. And again, here is a
ten-year comparison, we're going to compare the German, which is the orange, the UK green, and the
lighter orange is the U.S. 10-year Treasury note. You see the same thing happening here where the
German was making higher highs while the U.S. and UK ten-year notes were failing to make higher highs
in their yield. And the same thing is also that low was formed in the fall of 2010, we saw the German ten
year failed to make lower lows in yield while the U.S. Treasury made on its ten year basis made a lower
yield and the UK failed to make a lower yield also, and that was your sentiment shift to bullish.
And that we've been looking for buy programs on major currencies and sell signals, okay, in the dollar.
Okay, in other words, the dollar would have been declining all during this period here. And at this point
where we saw a major shift, okay, in the yield diverge, the sentiment now was going to send the dollar
bullish and then major currencies on the farm would be declining, so that would be a risk off scenario here
or a sell program from Forex. And this is a buy program, okay, where the current foreign currency markets
would be rallying in the U.S. dollar at this time period would be declining. Okay, so it just basically think in
terms to make it real easy for yourself, when the yields are going up, the foreign currency market should be
trailing in sympathy, okay, because they're gonna chase yield, and as the yields drop, the foreign currencies
are gonna chase that down as well. There won't be any demand for that foreign currency, you know you
have flight to safety in the form of going back to the dollar and buying bonds. Okay, so again there's an
inverse relationship between the bond market itself and the yield. As bonds would go up, the yields would
drop and vice versa. Okay, so if that's basically the panoramic view of a risk on risk off scenario and
determining buys and sell programs on the daily basis, and this is one really strong powerful way of
keeping yourself in sync with the macro economic view and you'll have to go through all this economic
data, this is visual for you, you can see it happening. Is every three to four months, just look for a shift to
occur, and when you start seeing these things occur on your own, the yield, okay, that's when you want to
be looking at your price charts and looking for maybe some top formations and some reversal scenarios.
Okay, and same thing in here. So we're using seasonal tendencies, cyclical analysis, and the yield analysis
where the market will always seek yield and if yields are dropping that's going to be bearish for major
currencies and bullish for the US dollar.

Key support and resistance. You got to know your key levels guys. If you don't have a firm grasp on support
and resistance, all this is going to be a waste of time because none of your signals are ever going to
confirm entry or exit unless they get to on these key levels. The cornerstone to your success in trading will
be your patience in determining high timeframe key support resistance levels and waiting for the set ups to
form at those same levels.
Once you have directional bias based on the major market analysis, you identify key price levels from the
60-minute chart up to the monthly. Nothing less than a 60-minute chart and ideally the daily basis is
optimal. Determine monthly, weekly, previous day's session highs and lows as well as pivot levels and
Asian range highs and lows.

Markets sentiment. You have to avoid the herd mentality. If everybody's doing it, it's probably not a good
thing in the futures market or Forex. It's usually probably petered out, so you want to be looking to trade
against the majority. Now when decided to take action in the market, it is important to remain on the side of
the market that is the minority. Looking for trades when the market is by all appearances bullish when you
want to short is ideal. And you might have already guessed it, when it looks bearish, do your buying.
This might sound rather obvious and elementary, but consider how little actually follow this mindset.
Consider the intraday Judas swing for example, this is a sentiment play by all standards and it's used to
quantify an entry in the opposite direction.

Market structure, hunt intermediate-term price swings. If you find all of your trading occurring at long to
intermediate return price swings, you're gonna find that your trading is absolutely mind-blowing. The ideal
setups for trading low risk, high probability swings is to limit your consideration to only trading in the
intermediate term highs for shorts and entering intermediate term lows for longs. Taking short term scalps
and/or day trades in the same direction is advised. Your trading plan is not to capture every move in the
market, just the easy low hanging fruit. Using daily, four-hour, and hourly market structure and nesting
fractals can be a wildly profitable price action approach. This is my core price action concepts.

Open interest and premiums. Now open interest reflects smart money activity. Now the open interest
inference is an example here. In this example, we're looking at the Swiss franc and what I've noted here is
that price had ran up, it's been an uptrend and we moved into a trading range. Okay, and if you look at the
circle here, that is open interest that cumulative line that goes along and all of a sudden drops off
precipitously, drops us to trade down. Okay, that is open interest declining while the market is in a range,
what do you think that translates to? Well this is a concept that I adopted from Larry Williams and it still
serves me well today. The open interest while in a market consolidation or range can tip the hand of the
smart money if you understand what to look for.
If the open interest drops off 20 percent or more rapidly, this is attributed to commercial short-covering. If
open interest increases 20 percent or more rapidly in a consolidation, this is commercial short selling.
Commercial traders are the largest hedgers in the marketplace and when you hedge, you're essentially
shorting the market, you're selling something you already have. So if open interest declines, that means
that they're lightening up aggressively on their shorts, why would they do that? Because they anticipate
price going higher. When you have a premium in the futures contracts, that means the nearby contract is
selling at a higher price than the next month out in the future, that is a premium. Okay, that means there's a
high demand for that particular commodity or currency. If you see open interest dropping off in that
scenario, this is a near-perfect trade scenario, you need to be buying.
And as a result, this was a mega trade. This is in 2010. If you look at your charts around the Fourth of July
in 2010, you can see how in the FX market the pair for the Swissy fell out of bed like a rock. This trade in
the futures market actually moved 2,300 points and it's at less than half of two months, and the open
interest dropped while in the range, it's support at 118 and it was in an existing trend already. And that's
absolutely mind-boggling profit. And if you have that type of scenario unfold in the marketplace and you see
a couple these happen a year, you can really absolutely catch some explosive price moves. So I would
counsel you to look at open interest in the futures markets on the pairs that you trade. The Swiss franc is
an example here, but it's not limited to that. It's you can use it in the dollar index, you can use in the
Canadian dollar, Australian dollar, British Pound, Japanese yen and even the Euro.

Higher timeframe directional bias. Maintaining a focus on the weekly, daily and possibly the four hour time
frames will keep your trading in the highest probable direction. Look for support or resistance on these
levels to determine where price is trying to reach to. If price is trading lower on the daily, look for an old low
or high in the left side of your chart to determine if there is time and price available for trade given that
direction. Reverse for looking for buy setups. The main point is to keep the higher time frame in your
trading, it really does pay off.

General market risk on or risk off. You want to start with the interest rates. Are we seeing yields dropping or
increasing? Is there weakness or strength in the stocks, commodity and Treasuries? Is gold and or oil
giving you any clues? Is the dollar index confirming recent highs or lows compared to the interest rates?
Are there any market reports that might change the current market tone? The more the other asset classes
confirm or negate your trade idea, the better. Risk off, dollar rallies. Risk on, it slides. It's that simple.

Anticipatory stage of analysis. This is the stage of analysis where you will spend the majority of your time. It
is on the weekly and daily timeframes. Since the trades are based on these higher time frames, there might
be a few days or so before a trade actually sets up, don't worry, that's a good thing. You want to track the
interest rates, you want to be paying attention to CoT commitment of traders reports, major market indices,
seasonal tendencies, key support resistance levels and intermarket analysis with correlated assets. This
stage of analysis is the foundation to your successful trading career.

Execution stage of analysis. This is the stage of analysis where you hunt setups based on the insights and
conclusions you arrived at in the anticipatory stage of analysis. If risk is on, look for setups to buy
currencies and confirm these setups with higher moving or poised to rally stock indices, CRB, oil, gold, and
SMT concepts. If risk is off, look for setups to sell currencies and confirm these setups with lower moving or
poised to decline stock indices, the CRB index, oil, gold, and SMT concepts.

Management stage of analysis. This is the stage that requires your concepts for trade management on
open positions. The management of stop-loss orders, limit orders, initial profit objectives and scaling later
portions. Your trading plan must have clear concepts and techniques for the process that you will adhere to
when managing open positions. This stage of analysis is crucial for consistency and having longevity in this
career. You can find wonderful setups, enter at optimal entry points and still miss manage the trade. And in
this state, you will find results bring with it loss and regret if you do not manage your analysis correctly.

Reactionary stage of analysis. This stage of analysis is seen where your trades are open and you suspect
or clearly see reasons to collapse the trade and cut your losses or take what profits you have open. There
must be clear parameters in your trading plan that will invoke an immediate trade termination and abort on
your original trade idea. This might be a result of a report or a news event, global catastrophe and/or you
realize you're simply human and essentially wrong. Imagine that, making a mistake.

Documentation stage of analysis. This stage of analysis is where you record in your journal what your trade
idea was based on, what tools you used and why. Screenshots and how you felt going into the trade, while
the position was open, and what made you nervous. The results of the trade should be documented and
reflection on the trade plan and was it adhered to from the beginning to end. Document any lessons
learned from the trade and what you would have liked to have done differently. Determine what leverage
and/or lot size your next trade will be using. Keep these records and refer to them when you experience a
period of drawdown and/or a string of losses. It will remind you that temporary losing streaks come and go.
